segments.s678-g97: بدو گفت بهرام کاینست راست — بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (preverb + object), placed before the verb as is common in older Persian; گفت is the past tense of 'say' but the word-list provided lacks the verbal sense here; بهرام is the speaker's name in the nominative following the verb (verb–subject order is normal in poetry); کاینست (که این است) is a fused relative/complementizer meaning 'that/this is', and راست is the predicate adjective 'true'. Together: 'To him Bahram said that this is true.'
segments.s678-g98: برین راستی پاک یزدان گواست — برین is the fused preposition + demonstrative (بر + این) meaning 'on/with this' or 'concerning this'; راستی is the noun 'truth' (object of the preposition); پاک 'pure' qualifies یزدان (Yazdān, the divine name) which functions as the nominative/agent; گواست is a present/stative copular-like form meaning 'is witness' (i.e. 'stands as witness'). Literally: 'Upon this truth the pure Yazdān is witness.'
segments.s678-g99: ولیکن کنون کار ازین درگذشت — ولیکن 'but' introduces contrast; کنون 'now' gives time; کار 'the matter/affair' is the subject; ازین (از + این) 'from this' marks what it has passed beyond; درگذشت here functions as 'passed on/was gone beyond' (the usual meaning 'to pass' or 'pass away' — the supplied senses focused on death, but in this context it means 'passed [the scope of what was said] / moved beyond').
segments.s678-g100: دل و مغز من پر ز تیمار گشت — دل و مغز 'heart and brain/mind' are conjoined; من is the genitive pronoun 'my' (possession follows the noun in older Persian: 'the heart of me' = 'my heart'); پر ز + noun means 'full of' (پر = full, ز = 'of/from'); تیمار 'care/worry' is the thing filling them; گشت is the past-tense/inchoative 'became'. So: 'my heart and mind became full of worry.'
segments.s678-g101: اگر مه شوم‌گر نهم سر بمرگ — اگر 'if' introduces a condition. مه here is an archaic/poetic form (not the modern pronoun) and in this line functions as the speaker's self-reference or emphatic particle; شوم‌گر (shum‑gar) is an adjective/epithet meaning 'ill‑fated' or 'ominous' (shum + suffix/particle), and سر بمرگ 'head to death' means 'the head be given/laid to death' (بمرگ = 'to death'). The clause is conditional: 'If I become accursed/ill‑fated and (so) place my head toward death...' (note: some forms here are archaic/poetic and packed together in Ferdowsi's metre).
segments.s678-g102: که مرگ اندر آید بپولاد ترگ — که is the complementizer/causal 'that/for'; مرگ 'death' is the subject; اندر آید = 'comes in / approaches' (اندر + آید); بپولاد is an instrumental phrase 'with/armed by steel' (ب + پولاد/پولاد = 'by steel' or 'steel‑clad'); ترگ is a concrete image 'rag/shred' used here metaphorically of the body or of a torn piece by the sword. Together: 'for death will come within, with a steel (blade) that rends like a rag.'
segments.s678-g1: چو + NP is a subordinating clause marker meaning 'when'; چوخواهرش therefore = 'when his sister'. بشنید is 3rd-person past 'heard' (the subject is خواهرش). کامد is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular perfect/simple past meaning 'came' (the actual grammatical subject appears after it). ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' modifying آمد/کامد.
segments.s678-g2: برادرش is the noun 'brother' with the 3rd-person possessive suffix 'his'. پر before a noun works like an adjective 'full (of)'; پر درد = 'full of pain'. زان is the preposition 'from that' and رزمگاه is 'battlefield' — so the whole phrase modifies the coming, 'he came, full of pain from that battlefield.'
segments.s678-g3: بینداخت is a 3rd-person past-tense verb 'threw/cast off'. In poetic word order the verb appears before its subject: آن نامدار is the following noun phrase 'that famed/renowned (one)' as subject. افسرش is the direct object with the possessive suffix 'his' and here means 'his helmet' (not 'officer').
segments.s678-g4: بیاورد is another 3rd-person past (literary) verb 'brought/caused to come'. فرمانبری is a noun 'obedience/submission' functioning here as the object; چادرش is a genitive phrase 'her veil' (چادر + possessive -ش) linked to فرمانبری, so the string reads roughly 'brought (the) obedience of her veil' — an idiomatic way to express that the veil was brought/produced in submission. Poetic ellipsis leaves the exact subject of بیاورد somewhat implicit.
segments.s678-g5: بیامد is 3rd-person past 'came'. بنزد is a prepositional/adverbial form meaning 'to the presence of / near'. برادر is 'brother' and دمان ('دمان') here means 'skirt/hem' — together the phrase indicates approaching the brother's side/hem (i.e. coming up to her brother). Word order places the verb first, as is common in poetic prose.
segments.s678-g6: دلش = 'her heart' (دل + possessive -ش). خسته is an adjective 'weary/tired' modifying دلش. ازدرد is a prepositional compound 'from pain' explaining the cause of the weariness. و links the two predicates; تیره روان is a two-word phrase where تیره 'dark/gloomy' modifies روان 'spirit/soul', so 'her spirit was dark/gloomy.'
segments.s678-g7: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial short form meaning 'to him' (بِ + او) — 'to him she said'. گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (poetic/epic past), introducing direct speech. کای is a vocative particle 'O' used to address someone; مهتر جنگجوی is the addressee title 'chief/leader, warrior' (مهتر modifying جنگجوی). Note: گفت is used as the verb 'said' in context.
segments.s678-g8: چگونه شدی is a two-word clause 'how did you become / how did this happen to you' (چگونه = 'how', شدی = 2nd-person past of شدن 'you became/you came to be'). پیش خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of Khosrow' and بگوی is a jussive/imperative present form 'tell / say' placed at the end of the line: 'tell (it) before Khosrow.' The verb-final order (چگونه شدی ... بگوی) is a typical poetic arrangement.
segments.s678-g17: چنین (thus) is an adverbial opening — ‘thus’ or ‘so’. گفت is past-tense ‘said’ (here introducing speech). داننده is a substantive ‘the knowing/learned one’ and خواهر names her as a ‘sister’ (vocative or appositive). بدوی is a descriptive name/epithet attached to خواهر (here treated as a proper or descriptive epithet ‘Baduy’/‘primitive/original’). Word order is typical Persian: adverb → verb → noun phrase naming the speaker.
segments.s678-g18: که‌ای is a clipped vocative/relative combination: که + ای, functioning like ‘O / who’ to call someone. پرهنر is an adjective ‘full of skill / cultured’. مهتر is a titular noun ‘chief/eldest/distinguished man’ addressing rank. نامجوی is a compound noun ‘name‑seeker’ (one who seeks a name/reputation). Together this is a vocative phrase addressing a distinguished, fame‑seeking person.
segments.s678-g19: تو را uses تو + the accusative/emphatic marker را to mark ‘you’ as the addressee/object. چند گویم is a rhetorical interrogative: چند ‘how often/how many times’ + گویم (1st‑person present/subjunctive) ‘shall I say/tell’. سخن is the noun ‘word/speech’. نشنوی is 2nd‑person present negative ‘you do not hear/listen’. The clause literally: ‘To you, how often shall I say (words), you do not hear.’
segments.s678-g20: به پیش آوری is a verbal phrase: به + پیش (toward/presence) + آوری (2nd‑person present of آوردن) ‘you bring/produce before (us)’. تندی و بدخوی are two coordinated nouns: تندی ‘haste/fierceness/rashness’ and بدخوی ‘ill‑temper, bad disposition’. Together they are the objects brought forward by the addressee’s conduct.
segments.s678-g21: نگر is the imperative ‘look! / behold!’. تاچه is a fused interrogative ‘what / to what extent’ (lit. ‘until/what’) introducing a content question. گوید is 3rd‑person present ‘says’. سخن گوی بلخ strings the noun ‘sokhan’ (speech) with گوی (here ‘speaker/sayer’) and بلخ (‘Balkh’) as a locator: ‘the speaker of Balkh’. The whole unit means ‘Look and see what the speaker of Balkh says.’
segments.s678-g22: که is the relative/complementizer ‘that’. باشد is the present/subjunctive form of ‘to be’ used to state the proposition (‘may be / is’). سخن گفتن is a noun phrase built from سخن + the verbal noun گفتن ‘speaking/utterance’. راست and تلخ are predicative adjectives: راست ‘true/honest’ and تلخ ‘bitter/harsh’ (figurative). Grammar: ‘that speaking the truth is bitter’ (i.e., truthful speech will be bitter).
segments.s678-g23: هرآنکس ‘whoever’ starts a relative clause; که is the subordinator ‘who/that’. آهوی تو is a possessive phrase ‘your āhu’ (āhu = ‘gazelle’, here a term of endearment / ‘beloved’). باتوگفت is the fused past form باتو گفت ‘he said to you / spoke with you’ (با + تو + گفت). The clause means ‘whoever, when he said “your gazelle” to you…’.
segments.s678-g24: همه is the universal quantifier ‘all’. راستیها (plural) is ‘truths’ or ‘truthful things’. گشاد is here an adjectival/past‑participle sense ‘open/laid open’ (from the idea ‘opened/exposed’). ازنهفت is an ablative phrase ‘from concealment / out of hiding’. Together they form the result clause: ‘all the truths were opened out of hiding.’
segments.s678-g41: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing reported speech; گویند is an archaic/neutral narrative present 'they say' (introducing what people said); چوبینه is the thematic noun 'the wooden one / cudgel' (here a concrete object) and بدنام گشت is a verbal predicate 'became disgraced' with گشت as the past/verb of becoming. Word order is narrative: subordinating particle + reporting verb + clause whose predicate (بدنام گشت) follows the noun.
segments.s678-g42: همه نام is a noun phrase 'the whole/entire name (i.e. reputation)'; بهرام is the proper name in apposition; دشنام گشت is the verb phrase 'became a curse/insult' (دشنام as noun 'curse, abuse' and گشت = 'became'). The construction places the subject/reputational phrase before the predicate, with no overt copula (گشت serves as the verbal predicate).
segments.s678-g43: برین is composed of بر ('on/upon') + ین (a literary form of 'این' = 'this'), so 'on this'; نیز هم is a double adverbial 'also / furthermore' (emphatic); خشم یزدان بود is a nominal predicate 'the anger of God was' with بود as the past copula. Overall: 'on this also God's anger was (upon him)'.
segments.s678-g44: روانت is روان + second-person suffix -ت 'your soul'; the two repetitions of به introduce locations ('into / in'): به دوزخ به زندان بود = 'was in hell and in prison.' Here بود is the past copula and the sequence lists parallel complements (locations) after the possessed noun.
segments.s678-g45: نگر is the imperative 'look/see'; تا here functions idiomatically with نِگر to introduce an exception or limit ('look — apart / see whether'); جز از means 'except from' or 'apart from'; هرمز شهریار is the name + title 'Hormoz the king/sovereign' in apposition.
segments.s678-g46: که is the relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing the relative clause; بد modifies درجهان (here read as 'bad/inferior in rank in the world' — a compact classical phrasing); مر is the enclitic 'me' (dative/benefactive), followed by تو را 'you' with the object marker را, and خواستار functions as a predicate/adjectival participle 'desirous / one who seeks' (so: 'who, low in rank in the world, sought you for me'). Classical Persian often places the beneficiary (مر) before the object.
segments.s678-g47: هم is 'also'; آن تخت و آن کاله is a paired noun phrase 'that throne and that fort/keep'; ساوه names the place Saveh and شاه is the title 'king'—together indicating 'the throne and the fort of Saveh (belonging to the king)'. The sequence lists possessions/locations before the verb.
segments.s678-g48: بدست آمد is the idiom 'came into (his) hand / was obtained' (بدست here 'in the hand' with آمد 'came' in past); و is 'and'; برنهادی is the past of برنهاد 'placed upon/put on' (transitive, subject omitted and understood from context); کلاه 'hat' is used metonymically for 'crown' (the object placed). The clause therefore reports acquisition and the placing of the crown, with the subject left implicit.
segments.s678-g65: نکردی is the past negative 2nd-person singular ('you did not do'). The following vocative جوان ('young man') addresses the addressee after the verb (a poetic inversion). جز برای تو کار groups the exception phrase: جز = 'except', برای تو = 'for you' (i.e., 'for your own benefit'), and کار = 'deed/work' — together: 'you did no deeds except for yourself.'
segments.s678-g66: ندیدی is the past negative 2nd-person singular of دیدن ('you did not see'). دلت is 'your heart' (دل + possessive -ت), here the logical patient of 'seeing' (i.e. 'your heart saw'). جز به روزگار is an exception/limiting phrase: جز = 'except', به = 'to/for' and روزگار = 'the times/fate' meaning 'you (or your heart) only looked to fortune/the changing times.' Word order (verb before object) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s678-g67: تن آسان بدی is a poetic phrase meaning 'you gave (your) body ease' → 'you lived an easy life.' بدی here is an archaic/poetic past 2nd-person form meaning 'you gave' (from دادن/دادى) rather than the noun 'evil' (so the provided noun senses don't match this verbal use). شاد و پیروزبخت ('joyful and fortunate') are predicative adjectives describing the state in which the addressee lived.
segments.s678-g68: چراکردی is a past interrogative form 'why did you do...'. آهنگ here means 'intention/aim' (object of the verb), and این تاج و تخت = 'this crown and throne' is the goal of that intention. Note the verb (چراکردی) precedes the object phrase, a common order in Persian poetry.
segments.s678-g69: تودانی is the archaic/poetic contraction of تو دانی, second-person singular present 'thou/you know'. که introduces a subordinate clause (here a relative/explanatory clause). ازتخمه اردشیر literally means 'from the seed/stock of Ardashir' (ازتخمه = از + تخمه, 'from the lineage of'), and it functions as the source phrase inside that clause.
segments.s678-g70: بجایند is an archaic 3rd-person plural verb meaning 'they take the place / they succeed / they are manifest' and here predicates the following noun; Persian allows the verb to appear before its subject (inversion). شاهان is the subject 'kings'. برنا و پیر are two coordinated adjectival descriptions 'young and old' modifying شاهان.
segments.s678-g71: This phrase is the repeated prepositional pattern با گنج و با لشکر بی‌شمار ('with treasure and with an innumerable army'). In the manuscript the preposition/conjunction may appear fused (e.g. 'ابا' / 'وبا'), but grammatically it is با + گنج and و + با + لشکر; بی‌شمار is a postpositive adjective 'countless'. The whole phrase states accompaniment/means.
segments.s678-g72: به ایران is a directional prepositional phrase 'into/to Iran'. که here introduces the relative/hypothetical clause 'who...'. خواند is a 3rd-person singular form meaning 'calls' / 'would call' (here used in a general/hypothetical sense). تو را is the marked object ('you' with the object marker را). شهریار is the predicative noun 'king' — 'who would call you king.'
segments.s678-g57: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. The sequence 'آشفته شد هرمز' is verb-final in the classical/poetic style: 'هرمز آشفته شد' = 'Hormoz became disturbed' (شد = past of 'to become'). The following prefixed verb 'وبردمید' (و + بردمید) is coordinated to the previous predicate and shares the same subject (هرمز); in older/poetic Persian such a compound/prefixed older-form verb means roughly 'and [he] withdrew / took himself off' and is attached directly after شد.
segments.s678-g58: 'به' introduces the cause/agency here and functions like 'at/by/upon' (classical use: 'in/at the words of'). 'گفتار' is a genitive noun 'speech/utterance' and takes the following name as its possessor: 'گفتار آذرگشسپ' = 'the speech of Azargashasp'. 'پلید' is a post-nominal adjective modifying آذرگشسپ ('the vile Azargashasp').
segments.s678-g59: 'تو را' is a fronted object/topic marker combination ('you' + را) meaning 'you' as the one affected. 'اندرین' = 'in this' modifies 'صبر' ('patience'). The verbal cluster 'بایست کرد' combines the older modal/auxiliary 'بایست' ('must/ought' or 'be obliged') with the past verb 'کرد' ('did/caused'), producing a sense like 'he made/compelled (you) to be patient' or 'he caused you to have to be patient' in this context.
segments.s678-g60: 'نبد' is an archaic/past negative copula meaning 'there was not / did not exist'. 'بنده را' is the noun 'the servant' (here a conventional humble or generic reference) marked by 'را' as topic/emphasis. 'روزگارنبرد' is a compound noun 'rokh-gār-e nabrad' compressed as one word here: 'war-time' or 'warlike age/period'. The clause therefore says there was no respite/absence of war for the servant.
segments.s678-g61: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'او را' marks the person affected ('upon him'). 'چنان سختی آمد' = 'such hardship came' with آمد as the simple past 'came/happened'. The verb 'بروی' (from رفتن/رو) here is an older/poetic form meaning 'he went away/left' and continues the narrative action of the clause.
segments.s678-g62: The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of از) = 'from' indicates origin; 'بردع' is 'cloak/mantle' (the place/from which something emerges). 'بیامد' is past 'came forth'. 'پسر کینه جوی' is a noun phrase where 'پسر' = 'a son' and 'کینه جوی' is a compound adjective 'kineh-ju' = 'seeking hatred' → 'vengeful' or 'revenge-seeking', i.e. 'a vengeance-seeking son.'
segments.s678-g63: 'ببایست' is an impersonal/modal form meaning 'it was necessary' or 'one must'. It governs the infinitive 'رفتن' ('to go'). 'برشاه' = 'to/upon the king' (direction or destination) and 'نو' is the postnominal adjective 'new' describing 'شاه'. The phrase therefore means 'it was necessary to go to the new king.'
segments.s678-g64: 'بکام وی' = 'to his desire / pleasing to him' (بکام as a prepositional/adverbial phrase + وی 'him'). 'آراستن' is the infinitive 'to adorn/prepare/equip'. 'گاه نو' 'a new occasion/time' is the object phrase (گاه = time/occasion, نو = new). The whole segment expresses 'to prepare a new occasion that would please him.'
segments.s678-g81: ندیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past of دیدن, meaning 'they did not see'. هرگز follows the verb here as an adverbial negator ('never'). سواری چوسام is the object phrase 'a rider like Sam' where چو‑ attached to the name (چوسام) means 'like/as Sam'.
segments.s678-g82: نزد پیش او is a doubled adverbial 'in front of / in the presence of him' (نزد and پیش both mark proximity/presence). شیردرنده گام is a compound descriptive phrase: شیردرنده ('lion‑tearing' / ferocious) modifies گام ('step'), forming an epic epithet 'with a lion‑tearing step'.
segments.s678-g83: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' here. نوذر شد is 'Nowzar became / fell' (شد past of شدن). از بخت بیدادگر is a causal/instrumental phrase 'from / under (a) cruel fate' where از introduces the cause and بیدادگر is an adjective 'cruel' modifying بخت ('fate').
segments.s678-g84: بپا اندر آورد is a verbal compound: بپا آوردن/بپا آورد literally 'to set up / to put into effect'. اندر is the locative/introducing preposition 'in/into', and آورد is past 'he brought/caused'. رای‌پدر is the object 'the father's counsel' (written together here), so the whole phrase means 'he put his father's counsel into action'.
segments.s678-g85: همه مهتران is the plural subject 'all the chiefs/leading men' (مهتران = elders/chiefs). سام را uses the definite object marker را, so 'they called/summoned Sam'—خواستند is the 3rd‑person plural past of خواستن meaning here 'they requested/summoned'.
segments.s678-g86: همان تخت پیروزه is a noun phrase 'that same (همان) turquoise (پیروزه) throne (تخت)'; پیروزه here describes the throne (ornament/stone or color). آراستند is the 3rd‑person plural past of آراستن 'they adorned', agreeing with the (implied/previous) plural subject.
segments.s678-g87: بران is a discourse connective 'thereupon / upon that' linking to the previous action. مهتران is the indirect object 'to the chiefs'. گفت is the past 'he said' and هرگز مباد is an idiomatic jussive negative 'may it never be' (مباد = 'may it not happen').
segments.s678-g88: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. جان سپهبد is a possessive/nominal phrase 'the life of the spahbed' (epic Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe). کند is the present/jussive verb 'may (he) make / may it become' used after مباد to form the wish/negation. تاج یاد is a topical compound 'a crown of remembrance' (تاج qualified by یاد).
segments.s678-g9: This is a conditional subordinate clause introduced by the particle 'گر' (= 'if'). Word order is subject (او) + adverbial (ازجوانی) + verb (شود) + predicate adjective (تیزوتند). 'ازجوانی' is an adverbial phrase ‘from/in youth’; 'تیزوتند' is a fused, somewhat archaic compound adjective meaning 'sharp and swift'.
segments.s678-g10: 'مگردان' is a negative imperative ('do not turn' / 'do not change direction') addressing 'تو' (you). 'درآشتی' is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'in (or to) reconciliation' and 'رای کند' is a verb phrase with 'رای' as 'plan/counsel' and 'کند' = 'do/make' (third person). Together they mean 'do not turn to make a plan for reconciliation' (i.e. do not attempt to reconcile).
segments.s678-g11: 'بخواهر' = 'to (his) sister' (به + خواهر). 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus/so'. 'گفت' is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech). The name 'بهرام گرد' follows the verb as the speaker's name/title; 'گرد' is part of the name/epithet.
segments.s678-g12: The conjunction/relativizer 'که' introduces the reported clause. 'او را' is topicalized object ('him' with the definite/object marker را). 'زشاهان' (ز + شاهان) marks the class/group 'of the kings' / 'among the kings'. 'نباید' is the impersonal modal 'should/must not', and 'شمرد' (infinitive/present form) means 'to count/reckon'. Together: 'that he should not be counted among the kings.'
segments.s678-g13: The repeated 'نه ... نه ...' constructs enumerate negative qualities. Here 'جنگی' is an adjective 'warlike/fighter' modifying 'سواری' ('rider, horseman'). 'بخشنده‌ای' is an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'a generous one' (the -ای suffix here forms 'one who is ...').
segments.s678-g14: Continues the 'نه ...' list. 'داناسری‌گر' is a compound agentive 'dānā + sarī-gar' meaning 'one who gives wise counsel' (a wise counsellor). 'درخشنده‌ای' again is a nominalized adjective 'a shining/illustrious one.' The final 'ای' marks the person-as-adjective nominalization.
segments.s678-g15: A comparative construction: 'هنر بهتر از گوهر نامدار' = 'art/skill is better than a famed gem.' 'هنر' is the subject ('skill/virtue'), 'بهتر از' marks the comparison ('better than'), and 'گوهر نامدار' is 'famed/renowned jewel' with the adjective following the noun (poetic word order).
segments.s678-g16: This is an independent clause: 'هنرمند' here means 'a skillful person' (one who has art/skill). 'باید' is the modal 'must/ought', and 'تن شهریار' literally 'the person/body of the king' idiomatically 'the king needs (such) a person' — object (تن) + title (شهریار). Word order is compact and somewhat elliptical (the verb 'has need' is implicit in 'باید').
segments.s678-g89: که introduces the clause ('that' / 'for'). "خاک منوچهر" is a genitive construction meaning "the earth/dust of Manuchehr" (noun + name). گاه here functions poetically as 'place' or 'station' (an older usage), and منست is a compressed, archaic/copulative phrase meaning 'is mine' / 'is my ...' (poetic contraction of 'من است'), so the whole segment says 'that Manuchehr's dust is my place'.
segments.s678-g90: سر تخت نوذر is an elided genitive chain meaning 'the top/summit of Nowzar's throne' (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe linking). کلاه منست again uses the poetic copula (منست ≈ 'من است') to mean 'is my hat/cap', so the clause says 'the summit of Nowzar's throne is my cap.' Word order is noun‑noun (سر تخت) + possessor (نوذر) without explicit ezāfe for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s678-g91: بدان گفتم is a fixed formula 'to that / to him I said' (بِ- + آن → بدان), used as 'I said (in response)'. این‌ای برادر is a vocative 'O brother' (این‌ای is an emphatic/formal 'this one' addressing the addressee). که introduces the following content clause about the throne.
segments.s678-g92: تخت نیابد مگر مرد پیروزبخت is a nominal clause with the verb نیابد (classical negative/impersonal form of 'to obtain/attain') meaning 'is not attained/obtained'. مگر introduces the exception 'except'. مرد پیروزبخت ('a fortunate/lucky man') is the only one who can attain the throne; adjective follows the noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s678-g93: که دارد introduces a relative clause 'who has/possesses'. The following nouns are the items possessed: کفی (literally 'palm' used metaphorically for generosity), راد ('generous' / noble), وفر ('divine glory/splendour'), و نژاد ('and lineage'). Items are listed in coordination; دارد is the present-tense verb 'has'.
segments.s678-g94: This continues the description with coordinated qualities: خردمند 'wise', روشن‌دل 'clear-/bright‑hearted' (compound adjective), and the predicate 'پر ز داد' = 'full of justice' where پر (full) takes the poetical preposition ز ('of') with داد ('justice'). The adjectives are enumerated with و (and).
segments.s678-g95: ندانم is the negative personal verb 'I do not know' (ن‑ + دانم). که introduces the subordinate clause. بر تو چه خواهد رسید is a future impersonal construction: خواهد رسید 'will reach / will befall', with چه as the interrogative 'what' and بر تو marking the experiencer 'to you'. The literal sense is 'I do not know what will befall you.'
segments.s678-g96: که اندر دلت شد خرد ناپدید: that 'in your heart' (اندر + دل + enclitic -ت for 'your') 'became' (شد) + 'wisdom vanished' (خرد ناپدید). The verb شد precedes the nominal predicate for poetic word order; literally 'wisdom became vanished in your heart' → 'wisdom has disappeared from your heart.'
segments.s678-g73: اگر is the conditional 'if' introducing the clause. شهریاری functions as the subject noun ('sovereignty' / 'a sovereign') and the prepositional phrase به گنج و سپاه ('by/with treasure and army') uses به in an instrumental sense ('by/with') to indicate the means; the whole segment sets up the agent and his resources before the verb. (Poetic word order places the conditional and NP before the verbal phrase.)
segments.s678-g74: توانست is the past ability verb 'was able/managed' and governs the following non-finite verbal construction. کردن here is the light/auxiliary verb combining with نگاه to make the compound verb 'نگاه کردن' ('to look'); به before ایران marks the target of the look ('towards/at Iran'). Note the poetic ordering: the finite verb توانست appears before the light verb and verbal noun rather than the more colloquial توانست به ایران نگاه کردن.
segments.s678-g75: نبودی is a past negative/existential 'there was not' (here used impersonally to mean 'there was none'); جز 'except' introduces the sole exception; از ساوه gives origin ('from Saveh'); سالار چین is a noun phrase 'the commander/lord of China' modifying the exception. The phrase therefore means 'there was no one except the commander of China from Saveh.'
segments.s678-g76: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the clause modifying سالار چین. آورد is the past tense 'brought' and لشکر is its object 'an army'; به ایران‌زمین is a prepositional phrase 'to the land of Iran' where به marks destination. Word order here is poetic but straightforward: relative clause with past verb follows the head noun it modifies.
segments.s678-g77: تو را uses تو 'you' plus the object marker را, marking you as the direct object. پاک یزدان is the nominative agent 'the Pure God' (adjective + divine name). بروبرگماشت is an archaic/poetic past compound meaning 'set/placed' or 'appointed' (here transitive), so the clause reads 'the Pure God placed/appointed you.'
segments.s678-g78: بد is an adjective 'evil/bad' functioning as a predicate modifier applied to او ('him') — literally 'he made him an evil.' ز here is the poetic form of از 'from/of' and introduces the scope/target 'ایران و توران' ('Iran and Turan'); بگاشت is the past verb 'he placed/appointed' (causative sense 'made/appointed him [to be]'). Together the line says he made him a bane/scourge with respect to Iran and Turan. The subject of بگاشت is contextually the same agent as before (God) or the narrative agent; word order is poetic but the verb at the end is the main action.
segments.s678-g79: جهاندار is the subject 'the world-ruler/sovereign.' تا here is a poetic particle meaning roughly 'even' or marking extent ('even this'), introducing این جهان 'this world' as the extent of the creative act; آفرید is the past tense 'created.' The clause therefore reads 'The sovereign even created this world.'
segments.s678-g80: زمین is the direct object 'earth/land' and کرد is the simple past 'made' (کرد + object = 'made the earth'). و هم 'and also' links the parallel clause where آسمان 'the sky/heaven' is the object and آفرید is the past 'created' — a parallel pair of creation verbs with coordination by و هم.
segments.s678-g25: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' placed before the verb phrase. رای ویرانی شهر خویش is the noun phrase object 'a plan/counsel of the city's destruction' — رای (plan/counsel) governs the genitive phrase ویرانی شهر (destruction of the city). خویش is possessive (formed from خوی + enclitic ش) giving 'your/one's own city.' Word order places the negative verb before the nominal object.
segments.s678-g26: ز گیتی چو برداشتی بهرخویش is a circumstantial clause giving motive/condition: ز گیتی 'from/for the world', چو برداشتی 'when/if you withdraw/take away' (چو = 'when/if', برداشتی = past/perfective or modal 'you take/you remove/you depart'), and بهرخویش 'for your own sake.' The clause explains the worldly reason; برداشتی here is a verbal action linked to the preceding warning.
segments.s678-g27: برین is the preposition + demonstrative 'بر + این' meaning 'about this/on this.' بریکی (an adjectival modifier) qualifies داستان as 'a brief/delicate tale.' داستان زد کسی uses زد in the idiom 'داستان زدن' = 'to tell a story' (زد as 'to sound/perform/tell'), with کسی as the subject 'someone.'
segments.s678-g28: کجا here is rhetorical ('what/where' → 'what share'), بهره بودش is 'a share/portion he had' with بودش the enclitic/past copula meaning 'he had/it was to him.' ز دانش بسی 'of much/abundant learning' (ز = 'of/from', دانش = 'learning/knowledge', بسی = 'much/enough'). The whole clause asks rhetorically what learning the teller possessed.
segments.s678-g29: This line strings contrasting clauses introduced by repeated که ('that/who' used here as a coordinator/explanatory particle). خر شد is a simple past 'became a donkey' (metaphorically 'became a fool'). که خواهد is 'who wants/desires' (خواهد = 'wants/desires'), زگاوان 'from/among the youths' (ز + گاوان), and سروی 'cypress-like' describing an idealized tall youth. The sequence lists different (foolish) behaviors.
segments.s678-g30: بیکباره is the adverb 'suddenly.' گم کرد is a causative/perfective construction where گم 'lost' is the state and کرد is the light verb 'made/caused/wrought' (here functioning as simple past 'lost/was lost'). گوش وبروی lists the affected items 'ear and face' (گوش often lit. 'ear' but also 'attention'; وبروی is the conjunction+element 'and face/countenance'), so the clause describes a sudden loss (of senses/attention/appearance).
segments.s678-g31: نکوهش مخواه is an admonition: نکوهش 'reproach/censure' as the object and مخواه the negative imperative 'do not desire/seek.' از جهان 'from the world' plus the idiomatic reduplication سر به سر gives 'from the whole world/entirely' (سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'completely/entirely' modifying از جهان).
segments.s678-g32: نبود is the past negative 'there was not/was none.' از تبارت 'from your lineage' (تبارت = 'your line/ancestry'), کسی 'anyone' and تاجور is predicative 'a merchant/trader.' The clause states that no merchant existed among the addressee's ancestry; word order is negative copula + oblique phrase + predicate.
segments.s678-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. ز + و here functions poetically as 'از او' (from/of him / as a result of) indicating source or cause. نامور is an adjective 'renowned'; گشتی is the 2nd-person past/ perfective form of گشتن used here as 'you became' (classical usage where گشتن = 'become'); اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in/within, جهان = world). The clause order is conjunction → source → adjective → verb → locative.
segments.s678-g50: بجویی is the present/imperative form built with the prefix بــ plus the verb-stem جوی- meaning 'seek' or 'you should seek' (here directed at 'you'). کنون is an adverb 'now'; گاه is a noun 'time/occasion'; شاهنشهان (plural) means 'emperors' or 'throne of emperors' (here an objective phrase: 'the time/throne of kings'). The sequence places the verb before its temporal modifier (classical poetic order) and expresses an exhortation.
segments.s678-g51: همه نیکوییها is the subject phrase 'all good deeds/things' (همه = all, نیکوییها = virtues/goodnesses). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source. یزدان‌شناس is a noun meaning 'one who knows God' / 'the pious/theologian' (here: the God-knower as source of goodness). The clause is a simple source-statement: 'All goodness is from the God-knower.'
segments.s678-g52: مباش is the negative imperative 'do not be' (classical prohibition). اندرین = 'in/among these' (اندر + این), تاجور is 'merchant/trader', and ناسپاس is an adjective 'ungrateful/thankless' that follows the noun. The full imperative clause addresses the addressee: 'Do not be among these ungrateful merchants.'
segments.s678-g53: برزمی is an adjective/adverb 'warlike' or 'in warfare' describing the action; که introduces the clause ('when' / 'that'); کردی is 2nd-person past 'you did' (you engaged/you made); چنین = 'thus/so'. کش here is from کشیدن 'to pull/ to bear/ to endure' used in a causative/participial sense, and مشو is the prohibitive 'do not become' / 'do not be' (negative imperative). Together the clause says roughly 'When you acted thus in war, do not be one who endures/suffers (in that way)'.
segments.s678-g54: هنرمند بودی is a predicative past: 'you were skillful' (هنرمند = skillful/artist, بودی = you were). منی فش مشو is an admonition: منی 'me' (object), فش 'press/oppress' (noun/verb stem), and مشو again the negative 'do not become' — so 'do not press/oppress me' or 'do not be one who presses me.' The negative imperative targets conduct toward the speaker.
segments.s678-g55: به دل is a locative phrase 'into/within the heart' (به = to/into, دل = heart). دیو را یار کردی is a transitive clause with را marking the definite object: 'you made the demon a friend/companion' (یار = companion, کردی = you made/did). همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle 'always/still' placed after the verb. Word order is prepositional phrase → object marked noun → predicate.
segments.s678-g56: به یزدان is 'before/in the presence of God' (به = toward/in relation to, یزدان = God). گنهگار گردی is a verbal phrase 'you became/turned into a sinner' where گنهگار = 'sinner' and گردی is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person verb-form meaning 'you became' (here used as a change-of-state verb). همی again is the emphatic/continuative particle. The clause states the moral consequence: 'you have become a sinner in God's sight.'
segments.s678-g33: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). نیستی is the second‑person singular negative present form 'you are not / you were not' used here in the protasis; در میان + این جوان is a prepositional noun phrase 'among these young men' that completes the condition. Word order places the verb (نیستی) before the prepositional phrase, as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s678-g34: نبودی is a past (counterfactual) form of 'to be' used in the apodosis — here rendering the sense 'would not have been' (past/counterfactual). The subject من follows the verb (verb‑first order). از marks cause ('because of' / 'on account of'), داغ تیره روان is an instrumental/causal phrase: داغ 'grievous wound', تیره 'bleak/ominous' qualifying it, and روان 'soul/spirit'.
segments.s678-g35: پدرزنده 'living father' is a compound noun; و links it to تخت شاهی 'the royal throne' (تخت modified by شاهی). بجای is a locative/adverbial word 'in the place / before (someone)' that will be completed by the verb in the next segment; the whole phrase names the things placed.
segments.s678-g36: نهاده is the perfect/past participle 'placed / put' serving as the main verb (subject omitted, as is typical). تو functions as the indirect/dative 'for you' (i.e., placed before/for you). اندر میان پیش پای is an adverbial phrase 'in the midst / directly before the (your) feet' specifying the location; note classical word order places these adverbials after the verb.
segments.s678-g37: ندانم = 'I do not know' (negative present). سرانجام این چون is a nominal phrase 'the outcome / how this (matter) came about' — چون here means 'how' or 'that it should be so' — and بود is the past copula 'was', completing the clause: 'I do not know how this ended.'
segments.s678-g38: همیشه 'always' sets the habitual/time frame. دو چشمم is 'my two eyes' (چشم + possessive enclitic ـم written here as separate token م). پر از خون بود is a predicate: پر 'full (of)' + از 'of/from' + خون 'blood', and بود 'were/was' — literally 'my two eyes were always full of blood.'
segments.s678-g39: جز از ... is a limiting construction 'except / other than'. درد و نفرین are the objects 'pain and curse'. نجویی is a verbal noun/verb from جست‌/جوی used here as 'you seek' (second person), and همی is the classical habitual particle meaning 'always / continually' — so: 'You seek nothing except pain and curse.' Note the object precedes the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s678-g40: گل زهر is a genitive/compound 'flower of poison' = 'poisonous flower'. خیره is an adjectival/participial manner 'stunned / staring' describing the state of the perceiver. ببویی is the second‑person present form of بوییدن 'you smell / you sniff' (the prefixed بـ is the older present marker); همی again gives habitual aspect. Literal order places object before verb: 'you (keep) smelling the poisoned flower, dazed.'

sentences.s678-s49: Bahram said to him, “This is true — upon this truth the pure Yazdān is witness.”
sentences.s678-s50: But now the matter has passed beyond this; my heart and brain have become filled with care.
sentences.s678-s51: If I become ill‑fated (accursed) and put my head to death — for death will come within, with a steel shred.
sentences.s678-s1: When his sister heard that, his brother came from the road, full of pain from that battlefield.
sentences.s678-s2: That famed warrior cast off his helmet; she (or he) then brought the veil in submission.
sentences.s678-s3: She came to her brother's side; her heart weary from pain and her spirit dark.
sentences.s678-s4: She said to him, 'O chief warrior, tell Khosrow how this happened.'
sentences.s678-s9: Thus spoke the knowing sister Baduy: “O skilled, foremost seeker of a name!”
sentences.s678-s10: How many times shall I tell you words if you will not listen? You bring before us only haste and ill temper.
sentences.s678-s11: Look — see what the speaker of Balkh says: that truthful speaking is bitter.
sentences.s678-s12: Whoever, when he spoke to you, called you ‘gazelle’ — all truths were laid open from concealment.
sentences.s678-s21: When they said the wooden cudgel had become disgraced, Bahram's whole name became an object of abuse.
sentences.s678-s22: On this too the wrath of God was upon him; your soul was in hell and in prison.
sentences.s678-s23: Look — apart from Hormoz the sovereign, who in the world of low rank would have sought you for me?
sentences.s678-s24: Also that throne and that fortress of Saveh (the king's) came into his hand, and he set the crown upon (himself).
sentences.s678-s33: O young man, you did nothing except act for your own sake; your heart looked to nothing but the passing fortunes of time.
sentences.s678-s34: You gave your body an easy life, were joyful and fortunate — why did you set your aim on this crown and throne?
sentences.s678-s35: Know that from the line (seed) of Ardashir kings arise, both young and old.
sentences.s678-s36: If you enter Iran with treasure and with countless armies, who will call you king?
sentences.s678-s29: When Hormoz became disturbed and withdrew at the words of the vile Azargashasp.
sentences.s678-s30: He made you keep patience in this; the servant had no respite from the warlike times.
sentences.s678-s31: When such hardship came upon him, he went away; from his cloak there came forth a vengeance-seeking son.
sentences.s678-s32: It was necessary to go to the new king; to prepare a new occasion pleasing to him.
sentences.s678-s41: They had never seen a rider like Sam stride before him with lion‑tearing steps.
sentences.s678-s42: When Nowzar fell under a cruel fate, he set his father's counsel in motion.
sentences.s678-s43: All the chiefs summoned Sam; they adorned that same turquoise throne.
sentences.s678-s44: Thereupon he said to the chiefs, 'May it never be that the life of the spahbed become a crown of remembrance.'
sentences.s678-s5: If he becomes sharp and quick from his youth, do not turn to reconcile with him or devise a plan for reconciliation.
sentences.s678-s6: Bahram-Gird thus said to his sister that he ought not to be counted among the kings.
sentences.s678-s7: He is neither a warlike horseman nor a generous man, neither a wise counsellor nor a resplendent one.
sentences.s678-s8: Skill is better than a famed jewel; the king needs a skilled man.
sentences.s678-s45: That the dust of Manuchehr is my dwelling, the summit of Nowzar's throne is my cap.
sentences.s678-s46: To that I said: O brother, the throne is won by no one except a fortunate man.
sentences.s678-s47: That (he) has a generous palm, splendour and lineage, wise and clear‑hearted, and full of justice.
sentences.s678-s48: I do not know what will befall you, for wisdom has vanished from your heart.
sentences.s678-s37: If a sovereign, by means of treasure and an army, could set his gaze upon Iran.
sentences.s678-s38: There was none except the commander of China from Saveh who brought an army to the land of Iran.
sentences.s678-s39: The Pure God set you in place; he made him a scourge for Iran and Turan.
sentences.s678-s40: The sovereign created this world; he made the earth and likewise created the sky.
sentences.s678-s13: Do not plan the ruin of your own city for the world's sake.
sentences.s678-s14: Someone told a brief tale about this; what share did he have of abundant learning?
sentences.s678-s15: Some became asses, some longed for cypress-like youths; suddenly he lost ear and face.
sentences.s678-s16: Do not seek reproach from the whole world; there was not a single merchant in your lineage.
sentences.s678-s25: When you became renowned in the world, seek now the time/throne of the emperors.
sentences.s678-s26: All goodness comes from the God-knower; do not be among these ungrateful merchants.
sentences.s678-s27: When you took the field in war thus, do not endure (suffer) so; you were skillful — do not press/oppress me.
sentences.s678-s28: You made the demon a companion in your heart, and you have become a sinner before God.
sentences.s678-s17: If you had not been among these young men, I would not have been (here) — I, because of the dark wound of the soul.
sentences.s678-s18: He set (or placed) your living father and the royal throne in place before your feet.
sentences.s678-s19: I do not know how this affair ended; my two eyes were always full of blood.
sentences.s678-s20: You seek nothing but pain and curse; you keep sniffing the poisoned flower, dazed.
